cmeAnalyses.note: Originally submitted on 2013-08-09T19:16Z by Dhanesh Krishnarao. Updated by Jenna Zink on 2014-07-08T14:10Z.

One coronagraph (SOHO) analysis with Stereo CAT tool, based on the location of the associated flare/post-eruptive arcades just behind the NE limb, with active region rotating to the limb in a few hours.

One-coronagraph analysis of the very faint shock of this CME with StereoCAT tool based on the location of the associated flare/post-eruptive arcades just behind the NE limb at the time of the flare, with the active region rotating onto the limb in a few hours.

note: The first one comes from AR1302 and goes to high latitudes in EUV (08:55-09:22Z).
The second one comes from AR1305 with dimming below the region in EUV (09:19-10:10Z), more equatorial.

This is a combination of Leila's measurements with measurements by Temmer et al. (2015), and Liu et al (2014).   For an ENLIL simulation in Bain et al (2015, submitted), this was used together with parameters dcld=8, and xcld=4.
Updated bulk measurement based on StereoCAT imagery using SOHO LASCO C3 and STEREO B COR2 imagery.
Shock measurement using SOHO LASCO C3 and STEREO B COR2 imagery in StereoCAT.
Measurement by Hyesook Lee with only STEREO-A and SOHO images available, triangulation not possible, source region was used for direction.

Triangulation using C2/C3, COR2B with scc_measure by Leila (see Baker et al, 2013, doi:10.1002/swe.20097).  With error bars: "The CME
was determined to have a three-dimensional speed of
2500 ± 500 km/s in the direction of 125°+15°/-5° longitude
and 2° ± 10° latitude (in HEEQ coordinates), and a full
angular width of 140° ± 30°."
Simulation:
predicted arrival= 2012-07-24T01:10Z
http://ccmc.gsfc.nasa.gov/database_SH/Leila_Mays_092313_SH_1.php

Triangulation measurements by Liu et al. (see http://dx.doi.org/10.1038/ncomms4481) using COR1B, COR2B, C2 C3.  Parameters are taken from Figure 4 at a height of 21.5Rs.  Latitude and width are not given.   " The speed of the CME leading
edge first increased to about 3,050 km/s  around 03:14 UT at a
distance of about 13 solar radii and then decreased to about
2,700 km/s."

This is the CME magnetic structure measurement by Temmer and Nitta (2015), Solar Physics 10.1007/s11207-014-0642-3.   With error bars:
CME Magnetic Structure: v_max=2270±420 km/s, lon=130°±5°, lat=5°±5°, face-on half-width=130°±5°, edge-on half-width 60°±5°, tilt=60°±5°
CME shock v_max= 2580±280 km/s
CME mass from COR2B ~1.5±0.5x10^16 g.
DBM gamma used for this event = 0.01x10^-7 1/km

note: Dimming then filament eruption from N5E140

note: long filament eruption starting ~2013-12-04T21:40Z in 193 and 304 from the northern hemisphere. The W side of the filament disappears by 23:00Z, and post eruption loops are visible only on the W side by 23:18Z in 193, and dimming is visible. This probably corresponds to the N CME seen in C2 at 23:12Z. The E side of the filament seems to have more material and erupts to the NE side, by around 2013-12-05T23:00Z-02:40Z (last piece). This probably corresponds to the NE CME seen in C2 at 00:12Z.

Log: Another CME appeared late in the day, and was first seen in LASCO C2 at around 2014-01-09T20:48Z.  It can also be seen in STEREO A and B, but not enough imagery was available to make measurements. However, the CME appears slow with a very high southern latitude, so it will likely have no effect. A two-ribbon C5 flare and dimming were observed from Region 12036 right near disk center starting around 19:30 UT.  There was a faint eruption seen to the north in LASCO starting around 21:28UT, the assumption is that is associated with the flare/dimming.  There was STEREO-B beacon data, but no clear eruption was seen (STEREO-A unavailable).  Speed was obtained using cone model reprojection using Lat/Lon shown.
